title: Ruling guide lines for calligraphy
subtitle: How to set up a consistent practice sheet in nib widths
excerpt: Guide lines give the broad-edge calligrapher a framework: baseline, x-height, ascender line, descender line. Without them, letters drift in height and the writing reads as uneven regardless of how well the strokes themselves are formed. This tutorial sets up a reusable guide sheet that can be placed under the practice sheet and seen through it.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on ruling and line arrangement. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

content.text: Guide lines are the invisible structure of consistent calligraphy. A reusable guide sheet, ruled once and placed under each practice sheet, removes the need to re-rule for every session. For cartridge paper at 130 gsm, the guide sheet lines are visible through the practice sheet in good light; for thicker paper, a light box brings them through.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, size 3 (or the nib being used for the practice session)
Tracing paper or cartridge paper, practice sheet

content.text: The move, ruling in nib widths

ladder. Load the nib with a small amount of ink. Holding the nib at 90° to the baseline (the flat edge is perfectly horizontal), make a stack of small square marks, each touching the one above, from the baseline upward. For a size 3 Mitchell at 4 nib widths x-height, four such marks gives the x-height measurement.
Mark the baseline and x-height line with the ruler and a sharp pencil. Rule across the full width of the practice sheet.
7 nib widths above the baseline (use the nib-width ladder: 7 marks stacked above the baseline gives the ascender line). For Foundational, the ascender rises 3 nib widths above the x-height line.
3 nib widths below the baseline. The g, j, p, q, y descenders should all reach this line.

Repeat the baseline-to-baseline spacing across the sheet at even intervals. That is baseline + x-height + space between lines. A comfortable line gap is about 2 nib widths above the ascender line before the next baseline starts.

Rule in dark pencil (not pen) so the lines are visible through the practice sheet but will not be confused with the written letters. The guide sheet is placed under the practice sheet at the start of each session and removed before any finished work is done.

attrs.body: Each nib size requires its own guide sheet. A size 3 Mitchell produces a different nib-width measurement than a size 0 or size 6. Make a separate guide sheet for each nib used regularly, labelled with the nib size and hand (e.g. 'Mitchell 3, Foundational').
attrs.title: Changing nib size

items.fix: Work in front of a window or use a light box. Alternatively, rule the guide lines directly on the practice sheet in very light pencil, rule once then erase lightly to leave a shadow.
items.cause: The practice paper is too thick. 130 gsm cartridge is usually see-through enough in good light, but some brands are heavily sized and look more opaque.
items.symptom: The guide lines are not visible through the practice paper

items.fix: Make the nib-width ladder from a single loaded nib without reloading between marks. If the ink runs out mid-ladder, restart the ladder from a fresh load and count only the marks from that load.
items.cause: The nib was reloaded between marks, giving different amounts of ink and thus different mark sizes.
items.symptom: The line spacing drifts across the sheet because nib-width marks are not consistent
